text: Hey everybody, today I'm presenting a robotic sigmoid colectomy for recurrent diverticulitis in a female patient that has had a prior hysterectomy and also recently had a failed colonoscopy, actually a couple of attempts because they could not navigate the sigmoid colon. But aside from that, I'm going to share with you that my wife had a baby yesterday, so that is super exciting, our second girl. And a little added bonus, she looks like me, so no concern there. Alright. I've been doing more and more of these cases with a lateral to medial just to get the sigmoid colon on a nice long mesentery because I'm not routinely anymore doing high ligations if I have enough length.

The surgical setup includes four ports: one at the left subcostal area using a Veress needle at Palmer's point, another at McBurney's point in the right lower quadrant with a twelve millimeter trocar, and a five millimeter assistant port in the right flank. The patient is positioned in a steep 25-degree Trendelenburg, leaning to the right. Extensive adhesions are visible in the pelvis, attributed to diverticular disease and previous hysterectomy, with a redundant sigmoid colon folded over itself.

The surgeon removes the last bits of scar tissue, revealing the redundant sigmoid colon folded on itself. The colon is pulled up out of the pelvis, and the proximal resection margin is defined. The surgeon creates a mesenteric window at this margin, carefully preserving the superior rectal artery while dissecting down the mesentery close to the bowel. The vessel sealer is used to achieve hemostasis as the surgeon approaches the rectal mesentery, preparing for the distal resection.

The surgeon administers three milliliters of ICG intravenously to assess perfusion, confirming good proximal and distal resection margins. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon performs a natural orifice extraction, initially cutting at the distal resection margin. Observing a narrow segment with remaining epiploica, the surgeon uses a rigid proctoscope and a 25mm balsizer to identify the need for further excision. The surgeon continues with the vessel sealer to remove an additional four to five centimeters of sigmoid colon, reaching the rectum for a better distal resection margin.

The surgeon straightens out adhesions before proceeding to the anastomosis. A 29mm EEA anvil is placed proximally through a small collotomy in the specimen portion of the proximal sigmoid colon. The surgeon uses ICG to ensure perfusion is adequate, and performs the anastomosis transanally, alternating between the robotic console and manual manipulation.
The surgeon separates the sigmoid colon mesentery from the colon, tubularizing the colon for easier transanal extraction.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and performs a leak test using a rigid proctoscope, ensuring the integrity of the connection. The test shows no leaks, indicating a successful procedure.
